---
title: "Session 10: Real-World Data Work"
subtitle: "R Programming Workbook"
author: "R Programming Course"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Learning Objectives"}
By the end of this session you will be able to:

1. Use `rvest::read_html()` to parse **static** HTML pages and extract tables, lists, and text
2. Use `RSelenium` to drive a real browser and scrape **dynamic** (JavaScript-rendered) pages
3. Decide which of the three web-data tools fits a task: `rvest` (static) â†’ Selenium (dynamic) â†’ REST API (preferred when available)
4. Query REST APIs from R using `httr2` and parse JSON with `jsonlite` + `purrr::pluck()`
5. Apply rate limiting and error handling for robust API and scraping workflows
6. Apply the full tidyverse toolkit to solve multi-step analysis problems
7. Build an end-to-end data pipeline from messy input to clean output
8. Handle common data quality issues (inconsistent text, mixed date formats, type mismatches)
9. Wrap a pipeline into a reusable function with `purrr::possibly()` for safe iteration
10. Use `pivot_longer()` + `group_by()` instead of `rowwise()` for scalable wide-to-long summaries
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(forcats)
library(lubridate)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(rvest)
library(httr2)
library(jsonlite)
library(tibble)
library(RSelenium)   # browser automation (only used in Part 1B; chunks set #| eval: false)

set.seed(42)
samples <- tibble(
  sample_id = sprintf("S%03d", 1:90),
  tissue = rep(c("liver", "kidney", "brain"), each = 30),
  condition = rep(c("control", "treated"), times = 45),
  sequencing_date = sample(seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-31"), by = "day"), 90),
  read_count_millions = round(rnorm(90, mean = 25, sd = 5), 1),
  batch = sample(c("B1", "B2", "B3"), 90, replace = TRUE),
  rin_score = round(runif(90, 6.5, 9.8), 1),
  qc_pass = rbinom(90, 1, 0.92)
)
```

## Warm-up: Window Rank Functions

Before we touch the web, a quick detour into a tidyverse skill you will reach for constantly: **window rank functions**. They answer questions like *"which sample has the highest read count?"*, *"which decile does this RIN score fall into?"*, *"top 3 per tissue"*. dplyr ships four of them; they all return integer ranks but **break ties differently**:

| Function | What it does | Behaviour on ties |
|---|---|---|
| `row_number()` | Assigns a unique rank 1, 2, 3, ... | Breaks ties arbitrarily â€” every row gets a unique number |
| `min_rank()` | "Competition" ranking (sports scoreboard) | Tied rows share the lowest rank; next rank **skips** |
| `dense_rank()` | Like `min_rank()` but no skips | Tied rows share rank, next rank is +1 |
| `ntile(n)` | Buckets values into n equal-sized groups | Used for quantiles, percentiles, deciles |

```{r}
samples|>
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),.by=condition
  )|>
    head(10)
```

By default ranks go from **smallest to largest**. Wrap the column in `desc()` to flip.

### Worked Example â€” Ranking samples by read count

We pick six samples and show all four functions side by side. Notice the deliberately constructed tie: `S001` and `S002` both have 25.0 million reads.

```{r}
ex_samples <- tibble(
  sample_id  = c("S001", "S002", "S003", "S004", "S005", "S006"),
  reads      = c(25.0,   25.0,   31.2,   18.7,   22.4,   29.8)
)

ex_samples |>
  mutate(
    rn = row_number(desc(reads)),
    mr = min_rank(desc(reads)),
    dr = dense_rank(desc(reads)),
    bucket = ntile(reads, 3)        # tertiles, low->high
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(reads))
```

**Read the output column by column:**

- `rn` (`row_number`) â€” `S001` and `S002` are tied at 25.0 but get **different** ranks (3 and 4). dplyr breaks the tie arbitrarily by row order. Use this when you need exactly N rows and don't care which tied row "wins".
- `mr` (`min_rank`) â€” Both ties get rank 3, then the next sample gets rank **5** (rank 4 is skipped). This matches what a real-world ranking does (two silver medals â†’ no bronze).
- `dr` (`dense_rank`) â€” Both ties get rank 3, but the next sample gets rank **4** (no skip). Use this when you want a clean 1..k category index, e.g. for joining or faceting.
- `bucket` (`ntile(3)`) â€” Each sample is placed into one of three roughly equal-sized groups by value. Bucket 1 = lowest tertile, bucket 3 = highest.

### Per-group ranking with `.by =`

These functions become much more useful when you ask *"top N per group"*. Combine with the `.by =` argument of `mutate()`:

```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(rank_in_tissue = min_rank(desc(read_count_millions)),
         .by = tissue) |>
  filter(rank_in_tissue <= 3) |>
  select(tissue, sample_id, read_count_millions, rank_in_tissue) |>
  arrange(tissue, rank_in_tissue)
```

This returns the top three samples by read count **within each tissue** â€” a very common bioinformatics QC pattern.

::: {.callout-tip title="`row_number()` vs `slice_max()`"}
For "top N rows" you can also use `slice_max(read_count_millions, n = 3)`. Under the hood, `slice_max()` uses `row_number()`. Use the verb form when you want clean code, the explicit `mutate(row_number(...))` when you need the rank as a column for further analysis.
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 1: Top 5 by RIN score"}
Using the `samples` tibble, return the five samples with the **highest** RIN score, with a column `rank` showing 1â€“5. Use `row_number()` so each gets a unique rank even if two samples tie.

```{r}
samples|>
  mutate(
    rank="1â€“5",
    id=row_number(desc(rin_score))
  ) |>
    arrange(id) |> head(5)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code: add a rank column with row_number(desc(rin_score)),
# then keep only ranks 1:5 and arrange by rank.
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(rank = row_number(desc(rin_score))) |>
  filter(rank <= 5) |>
  select(rank, sample_id, tissue, rin_score) |>
  arrange(rank)
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 2: Top sample per tissue"}
For each tissue, return the single sample with the highest read count. Use `min_rank()` plus `.by = tissue` so that ties (if any) all surface together.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code: rank within tissue, keep rank == 1
```

```{r}
samples|>
  mutate(
    rank = min_rank(read_count_millions),.by=tissue
  )|>
    filter(rank==1)
```
::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(rank = min_rank(desc(read_count_millions)), .by = tissue) |>
  filter(rank == 1) |>
  select(tissue, sample_id, read_count_millions) |>
  arrange(tissue)
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 3: `min_rank` vs `dense_rank` on ties"}
Run both `min_rank()` and `dense_rank()` on the small tied vector below and compare. Explain in one sentence when youâ€™d pick which.

```{r}
ties <- tibble(score = c(95, 90, 90, 85, 80, 80, 80, 70))

# Your code: add columns mr and dr, print
```

```{r}
ties |>
  mutate(
    mr = min_rank(desc(score)),
    dr = dense_rank(desc(score))
  )
```
::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
ties |>
  mutate(
    mr = min_rank(desc(score)),
    dr = dense_rank(desc(score))
  )
# min_rank gives 1, 2, 2, 4, 5, 5, 5, 8  -> ranks reflect "how many beat me"
# dense_rank gives 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5 -> consecutive integers, no gaps
# Pick min_rank for a fair scoreboard; dense_rank for a category index.
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 4: Read-count deciles with `ntile()`"}
Add a column `decile` to `samples` that puts each sample into one of 10 buckets by `read_count_millions` (1 = lowest 10%, 10 = highest 10%). Then count how many samples land in each decile per tissue.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code: ntile(read_count_millions, 10) ungrouped, then count per decile x tissue
```

```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(
    decile = ntile(read_count_millions,n=10)
  )|>
    count(decile,tissue)
```
::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(decile = ntile(read_count_millions, 10)) |>
  count(tissue, decile) |>
  arrange(decile, tissue)
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 5: Per-batch quartiles"}
For each batch (`B1`, `B2`, `B3`) split that batchâ€™s samples into quartiles by `rin_score`. Add a column `rin_quartile` (1â€“4). Confirm with `count(batch, rin_quartile)` that each batch has roughly equal numbers per quartile.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code: ntile() inside mutate() with .by = batch
```

```{r}
samples|>
  mutate(
    quartile= ntile(rin_score,n=4),.by=batch
  )|>
    count(batch,quartile)
```
::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
samples |>
  mutate(rin_quartile = ntile(rin_score, 4), .by = batch) |>
  count(batch, rin_quartile) |>
  arrange(batch, rin_quartile)
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Common pitfall"}
`ntile()` does **not** guarantee equal-width bins â€” it guarantees equal-**count** bins. If you need value cutpoints (e.g., "below 7.5"), use `cut()` or `case_when()` instead. Reach for `ntile()` only when you want each bucket to contain the same number of rows.
:::

## Part 1: Working with Web Data

There are three ways to pull data off the web in R. Pick the lightest tool that gets the job done:

| Tool | When to use | What it costs |
|---|---|---|
| `rvest::read_html()` | Static HTML pages (table is in the source) | Cheap, fast, no extra setup |
| `RSelenium` (or `chromote`) | Dynamic pages where JavaScript builds the content | Needs a browser process running in the background |
| REST API (`httr2`) | Whenever the source provides one | Needs an API â€” but most bioinformatics databases do |

The rule of thumb is simple: **try the API first, fall back to static scraping, and only use a headless browser when you really must**. We will look at all three, in order from cheapest to most expensive.

### Part 1A: Static Scraping with `rvest`

`rvest` is built around three steps:

1. `read_html(url)` â€” download and parse the HTML into a tree.
2. `html_elements(tree, css)` â€” select nodes with a CSS selector.
3. `html_text2()` / `html_table()` â€” extract text or tables from the selected nodes.

That is the whole API. Once you can read CSS selectors, you can scrape almost any static page.

#### Worked Example 1 â€” Model organism reference table

We have a small HTML table comparing genome statistics across model organisms. We want it as a tibble with numeric columns (the source has commas inside numbers, so we have to clean them).

```{r}
ref_html <- read_html('
<table class="ref-table">
  <tr><th>Organism</th><th>Genome size (Mb)</th><th>Protein-coding genes</th></tr>
  <tr><td>Human</td><td>3,200</td><td>20,000</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Mouse</td><td>2,700</td><td>23,000</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Zebrafish</td><td>1,400</td><td>26,000</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Fruit fly</td><td>180</td><td>13,600</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Yeast</td><td>12</td><td>6,000</td></tr>
</table>')

genome_ref <- ref_html |>
  html_table() |>          # finds every <table> on the page; returns a list
  pluck(1) |>              # take the first (and only) one
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(across(c(`Genome size (Mb)`, `Protein-coding genes`),
                \(x) str_remove_all(x, ",") |> as.integer()))

genome_ref
```

**What each line does:**

- `read_html()` accepts either a URL or a literal HTML string. We use a string here so the example is reproducible without an internet connection.
- `html_table()` walks the parse tree, finds every `<table>` node, and returns a list of tibbles â€” one per table. With one table on the page, we just take the first.
- `mutate(across(...))` cleans the comma-separated numbers. Static scraping nearly always needs a cleaning step like this; the page renders the data for humans, not for R.

::: {.callout-tip title="Reading CSS selectors"}
`html_elements("table")` selects every `<table>`. `html_elements("table.ref-table")` only the one with class `ref-table`. `html_elements("td:nth-child(2)")` the second `<td>` of every row. The browser's "Inspect" tool shows you the selector for any element.
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise A: Chromosome Length Table"}
Scrape the inline HTML below. The result should be a tibble with two numeric columns (`length_mb` and `gene_count`) and one character column (`chromosome`).

```{r}
chrom_html <- read_html('
<table>
  <tr><th>Chromosome</th><th>Length (Mb)</th><th>Genes</th></tr>
  <tr><td>chr1</td><td>249</td><td>2058</td></tr>
  <tr><td>chr2</td><td>242</td><td>1309</td></tr>
  <tr><td>chrX</td><td>155</td><td>804</td></tr>
  <tr><td>chrY</td><td>57</td><td>63</td></tr>
</table>')

# Your code: parse the table and end up with numeric columns
```

::::

```{r}
library(janitor)
tmp <- read_html("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings")|>
  html_table()|>
  pluck(2)|>
  clean_names()|>
  as_tibble(.name_repair="unique") |>
  slice(-1)


tmp |>
  mutate(
    floor=str_extract(floors, "^\\d+")|> as.integer(),
    height = str_extract(height_14_15_2, "^\\d+")|> as.integer()
  )|>
  summarise(
    avg_height= mean(height, na.rm=TRUE),
    avg_floor = mean(floor, na.rm=TRUE)
  )

tmp1 <-  tmp|>
    select(floors)|>
    as.vector() |>
    unlist()

v <- tmp1[1:3]  
v
str_extract_all(v,"\\d+") |> sapply(get_all_floors)

get_all_floors <- function(x){
  x |> str_extract_all("\\d+") |>unlist() |> as.integer() |> sum(na.rm=TRUE)
}
```
::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}

```{r}
tmp |>
  select(height_14_15_2,floors)|>
  rename(height = height_14_15_2)|>
  rowwise()|>
  mutate(floors_1 = get_all_floors(floors))
```
```{r}
chrom_html |>
  html_table() |>
  pluck(1) |>
  as_tibble() |>
  rename(chromosome = Chromosome,
         length_mb = `Length (Mb)`,
         gene_count = Genes) |>
  mutate(length_mb = as.numeric(length_mb),
         gene_count = as.integer(gene_count))
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise B: Gene List from a `<ul>`"}
Not all data sits in tables. Sometimes itâ€™s a list. Pull the gene symbols out of the `<ul>` below and return a character vector.

Hint: `html_elements("li")` selects every list item; `html_text2()` extracts trimmed text.

```{r}
panel_html <- read_html('
<ul id="gene-panel">
  <li>TP53</li>
  <li>BRCA1</li>
  <li>EGFR</li>
  <li>MYC</li>
</ul>')

# Your code: return c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR", "MYC")
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
panel_html |>
  html_elements("ul#gene-panel li") |>
  html_text2()
```
:::

### Part 1B: Dynamic Scraping with `RSelenium`

Static scraping fails the moment the page builds itself with JavaScript. If you `read_html()` such a page, the table you want is not in the source â€” the browser would have created it after running the scripts. For these cases we need a real browser, driven by code. That is what **Selenium** does.

Selenium is the industry standard for browser automation. `RSelenium` is the R wrapper. The architecture has two parts:

1. A **Selenium server** (a Java process) that controls a real browser (Firefox/Chrome).
2. **R code** that sends commands to the server over HTTP.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# One-time setup: rsDriver() downloads geckodriver and starts the server.
# It returns both a server handle and a remoteDriver client.
rD <- rsDriver(browser = "chrome", port = 4446L, chromever = "latest")
  remDr <- rD$client
  remDr$navigate("https://example.com")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Setup notes (no Docker)"}
`rsDriver()` will try to download a matching `geckodriver` (Firefox) or `chromedriver` (Chrome) on first call. The most common failure is a **version mismatch** between your installed browser and the driver. If `rsDriver()` complains, run `binman::list_versions("geckodriver")` to see whatâ€™s cached, or download the matching driver manually from the Mozilla / Google releases page and place it on `PATH`.

You also need **Java** installed (Selenium server is a JAR). On Windows: install from <https://adoptium.net/>.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="2026 alternative: `selenider` and `selenium`"}
`RSelenium` was archived from CRAN in 2024 (it still works via `remotes::install_github("ropensci/RSelenium")`). The modern replacements are the `selenium` package (Selenium 4 with auto driver management) and `selenider` (a more Playwright-like DSL on top). The concepts are identical â€” `navigate`, `findElement`, `clickElement` â€” so what you learn here ports directly.
:::

#### Worked Example 2 â€” A page that needs JavaScript

We will build a tiny HTML page that injects a table only **after** a script runs, then prove that:

1. Static `read_html()` fails (the table is not in the source).
2. RSelenium succeeds (the browser executes the script first).

```{r}
#| eval: false
# A self-contained data: URL with an inline <script> that builds a table on load.
page <- "data:text/html,
<html><body>
  <div id='target'>loading...</div>
  <script>
    setTimeout(function() {
      document.getElementById('target').innerHTML =
        '<table id=\"genes\"><tr><th>Symbol</th><th>Length</th></tr>' +
        '<tr><td>TP53</td><td>2512</td></tr>' +
        '<tr><td>BRCA1</td><td>5592</td></tr></table>';
    }, 500);
  </script>
</body></html>"

# 1. Static scraping FAILS â€” the table never existed in the source.
read_html(page) |> html_elements("table") |> length()
#> 0

# 2. Selenium loads the page, JS runs, then we read the rendered DOM.
remDr$navigate(page)
Sys.sleep(1)                          # give the script time to run

rendered <- remDr$getPageSource()[[1]]   # the DOM after JS has executed
genes <- read_html(rendered) |>
  html_element("table#genes") |>
  html_table()

genes
```

**What each line does:**

- `remDr$navigate(url)` tells the browser to load the page â€” identical to typing the URL into the address bar.
- `Sys.sleep(1)` is a crude wait. In production code you would poll for an element with `findElements()` until it appears, but for didactic purposes a fixed sleep is enough.
- `remDr$getPageSource()` returns the **post-JavaScript** HTML. We feed it back into `read_html()` and use the same `rvest` selectors as before. The pattern is: *use Selenium to render, use rvest to parse.*

::: {.callout-important title="Always close the browser"}
Selenium leaves a real Firefox/Chrome process running until you stop it. Wrap your scraping in `tryCatch(..., finally = remDr$close())` and at the end of the session call `driver$server$stop()`. Forgotten browsers eat RAM and lock files.
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise C: Screenshot a Page"}
Using the existing `remDr` from above, navigate to a simple page and save a screenshot to `shot.png`. Look at `?remoteDriver` â€” the relevant method is `screenshot(file = ...)`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code:
# 1. remDr$navigate("data:text/html,<h1>Hello bioinformatics</h1>")
# 2. remDr$screenshot(file = "shot.png")
# 3. Confirm the file exists with file.exists("shot.png")
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
remDr$navigate("data:text/html,<h1>Hello bioinformatics</h1>")
remDr$screenshot(file = "shot.png")
file.exists("shot.png")  # TRUE
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise D: Click a Button to Reveal Data"}
The page below hides a gene table behind a button. Use Selenium to click the button, wait, and then read the table.

Hint: `remDr$findElement("css", "button#load")` selects the element; `$clickElement()` clicks it.

```{r}
#| eval: false
hidden_page <- "data:text/html,
<html><body>
  <button id='load' onclick=\"document.getElementById('out').innerHTML=
    '<table id=t><tr><th>Gene</th><th>FC</th></tr><tr><td>MYC</td><td>2.3</td></tr></table>'
  \">Show data</button>
  <div id='out'></div>
</body></html>"

# Your code:
# 1. navigate to hidden_page
# 2. find the button and click it
# 3. wait, then read table#t into a tibble
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
remDr$navigate(hidden_page)
remDr$findElement("css", "button#load")$clickElement()
Sys.sleep(0.5)

read_html(remDr$getPageSource()[[1]]) |>
  html_element("table#t") |>
  html_table()
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise E: Combine Selenium + REST API"}
A page lists Ensembl gene IDs in a `<ul>`. Use Selenium to read the IDs, then call the Ensembl REST API (covered in 1C below) for each one to fetch the gene symbol.

This exercise mirrors a real workflow: you discover identifiers on one site, then enrich them from another.

```{r}
#| eval: false
list_page <- "data:text/html,
<ul id='ids'>
  <li>ENSG00000141510</li>
  <li>ENSG00000012048</li>
  <li>ENSG00000146648</li>
</ul>"

# Your code:
# 1. navigate; extract the three IDs from the <li> elements
# 2. for each ID, call the Ensembl /lookup/id endpoint
# 3. return a tibble with columns ensembl_id, symbol
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
remDr$navigate(list_page)

ids <- read_html(remDr$getPageSource()[[1]]) |>
  html_elements("ul#ids li") |>
  html_text2()

fetch_symbol <- function(id) {
  resp <- request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_perform()
  tibble(ensembl_id = id, symbol = resp_body_json(resp)$display_name)
}

map(ids, fetch_symbol) |> list_rbind()
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise F: Defensive Scraping"}
Real pages are slow, drivers crash, networks hiccup. Wrap a scrape in `tryCatch()` so a failure does not leak a running browser process. Use a polling loop instead of a fixed `Sys.sleep()` â€” try `findElements()` up to 10 times, half a second apart, and return early when the element exists.

```{r}
#| eval: false
slow_page <- "data:text/html,
<div id='target'>loading...</div>
<script>setTimeout(function(){
  document.getElementById('target').innerHTML='<span class=ok>READY</span>';
}, 1500);</script>"

# Your code: navigate, poll for span.ok, read text, ALWAYS close on error
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
result <- tryCatch({
  remDr$navigate(slow_page)

  # Poll: try up to 10 times, 500 ms apart
  for (i in 1:10) {
    found <- remDr$findElements("css", "span.ok")
    if (length(found) > 0) break
    Sys.sleep(0.5)
  }

  if (length(found) == 0) stop("element never appeared")
  found[[1]]$getElementText()[[1]]
},
error = function(e) {
  message("scrape failed: ", conditionMessage(e))
  NA_character_
})

result   # "READY" on success, NA on failure
# When the whole script is done:
# remDr$close(); driver$server$stop()
```
:::

### Part 1C: REST APIs

Most public bioinformatics databases (NCBI, Ensembl, UniProt, Reactome, ChEMBL) expose a **REST API**. APIs are far more stable than HTML pages â€” the maintainers version them and announce deprecations â€” so prefer them over scraping whenever they exist.

The workflow with `httr2` is always the same:

1. Build a **request** â€” URL, headers, query parameters.
2. **Perform** the request.
3. **Parse** the response body â€” JSON into an R list, then into a tibble.

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `request(url)` | Start building a request |
| `req_headers(...)` | Add HTTP headers (e.g., `Accept`) |
| `req_url_query(...)` | Append query parameters |
| `req_throttle(rate)` | Limit requests per second |
| `req_error(is_error = ...)` | Control what counts as an error |
| `req_perform()` | Execute the request |
| `resp_body_json()` | Parse JSON response body |
| `resp_status()` | Get HTTP status code |

### Ensembl REST API â€” Gene Lookup

The Ensembl REST API gives programmatic access to gene annotations, sequences, variants, and more. No login required.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Look up BRCA2 (ENSG00000139618) â€” expand=1 returns transcript details
resp <- request("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/ENSG00000139618") |>
  req_url_query(expand = 1) |>
  req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
  req_perform()

brca2 <- resp |> resp_body_json()

# Inspect key fields
tibble(
  gene_id    = brca2$id,
  symbol     = brca2$display_name,
  biotype    = brca2$biotype,
  chromosome = brca2$seq_region_name,
  start      = brca2$start,
  end        = brca2$end,
  strand     = brca2$strand
)
```

The `req_url_query()` call appends `?expand=1` to the URL safely. The `req_headers()` call tells Ensembl we want JSON (not XML). `resp_body_json()` returns a nested R list; we then pull out named elements manually or with `purrr::pluck()`.

### UniProt REST API â€” Protein Metadata

UniProt provides protein sequences, annotations, and cross-references. The `.json` suffix on the URL is all that is needed.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# BRCA2 protein (UniProt accession P38398)
uniprot_resp <- request("https://rest.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/P38398.json") |>
  req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
  req_perform()

protein <- uniprot_resp |> resp_body_json()

# Extract protein name (nested several levels deep)
protein_name <- protein |> pluck("proteinDescription", "recommendedName", "fullName", "value")

# Sequence length
seq_length <- protein |> pluck("sequence", "length")

# PDB cross-references â€” each entry is a list; we pull xrefId values
pdb_refs <- protein |>
  pluck("uniProtKBCrossReferences") |>
  keep(\(x) x$database == "PDB") |>
  map_chr(\(x) x$id)

tibble(
  protein_name = protein_name,
  seq_length   = seq_length,
  n_pdb_structures = length(pdb_refs)
)
```

`purrr::pluck()` is the safe way to drill into nested lists â€” it returns `NULL` (not an error) if a key is missing. `keep()` filters list elements by a predicate. `map_chr()` extracts a character vector from a list column.

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 1: Ensembl Gene Lookup"}
Make API calls to the Ensembl REST API for three genes and return a single tibble. The Ensembl IDs are:

- TP53: `ENSG00000141510`
- BRCA1: `ENSG00000012048`
- EGFR: `ENSG00000146648`

Use `purrr::map()` to iterate over the IDs. Your tibble should have columns: `gene_id`, `symbol`, `biotype`, `chromosome`, `start`, `end`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
gene_ids <- c(
  TP53  = "ENSG00000141510",
  BRCA1 = "ENSG00000012048",
  EGFR  = "ENSG00000146648"
)

# Your code here â€” map over gene_ids and build a tibble
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
fetch_gene <- function(ensembl_id) {
  resp <- request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", ensembl_id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_perform()
  g <- resp_body_json(resp)
  tibble(
    gene_id    = g$id,
    symbol     = g$display_name,
    biotype    = g$biotype,
    chromosome = g$seq_region_name,
    start      = g$start,
    end        = g$end
  )
}

gene_annotations <- map(gene_ids, fetch_gene) |> list_rbind()
gene_annotations
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 2: JSON Parsing with `pluck`"}
Using the UniProt REST API, fetch the record for BRCA2 (accession `P38398`) and extract:

1. The recommended full protein name
2. The sequence length
3. A character vector of all PDB cross-reference IDs

The URL is `https://rest.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/P38398.json`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Fetch and parse
protein_raw <- request("https://rest.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/P38398.json") |>
  req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
  req_perform() |>
  resp_body_json()

# Your code: extract the three items above using pluck() and keep()
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
# 1. Recommended full name
prot_name <- protein_raw |>
  pluck("proteinDescription", "recommendedName", "fullName", "value")

# 2. Sequence length
seq_len <- protein_raw |> pluck("sequence", "length")

# 3. PDB IDs
pdb_ids <- protein_raw |>
  pluck("uniProtKBCrossReferences") |>
  keep(\(x) x$database == "PDB") |>
  map_chr(\(x) x$id)

cat("Protein:", prot_name, "\nLength:", seq_len, "aa\nPDB structures:", length(pdb_ids), "\n")
head(pdb_ids, 10)  # first 10
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 3: Rate Limit Handling"}
Ensembl's REST API asks clients to stay below 15 requests per second (the default limit). When querying many genes in a loop, use `req_throttle()` to be a good citizen.

Wrap a 10-gene query batch with a throttle of 5 requests per second. Use the `gene_ids` vector below.

```{r}
#| eval: false
many_ids <- c(
  "ENSG00000141510", "ENSG00000012048", "ENSG00000146648",
  "ENSG00000139618", "ENSG00000157764", "ENSG00000136997",
  "ENSG00000171862", "ENSG00000133703", "ENSG00000126814",
  "ENSG00000181143"
)

# Your code: build a throttled fetch function and map over many_ids
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
# req_throttle(rate) sets a cap on requests per second
fetch_gene_throttled <- function(ensembl_id) {
  request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", ensembl_id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_throttle(rate = 5 / 1) |>   # max 5 req/s
    req_perform() |>
    resp_body_json()
}

results <- map(many_ids, fetch_gene_throttled)
map_chr(results, "display_name")
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 4: API Error Handling"}
What happens if you query a gene ID that does not exist? By default `httr2` throws an error on HTTP 4xx responses. Add graceful error handling.

Call the Ensembl API with a bad ID `"ENSG99999999999"`. Use `req_error(is_error = \(r) FALSE)` to suppress automatic errors, then inspect the status with `resp_status()`. Return `NA` when the status is not 200.

```{r}
#| eval: false
bad_id <- "ENSG99999999999"

# Your code: perform the request, check status, return NA if not 200
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
safe_fetch <- function(ensembl_id) {
  resp <- request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", ensembl_id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_error(is_error = \(r) FALSE) |>   # never throw â€” we check manually
    req_perform()

  if (resp_status(resp) != 200L) {
    message("Bad status ", resp_status(resp), " for ", ensembl_id)
    return(NULL)
  }
  resp_body_json(resp)
}

result <- safe_fetch("ENSG99999999999")
is.null(result)  # TRUE â€” graceful failure
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 5: Joining API Data with Local Metadata"}
You have a local tibble of differential expression results (`study_genes`) and you want to enrich it with Ensembl gene annotations fetched via the API.

Fetch annotations for the 5 gene IDs, build a tibble, then `left_join` it with `study_genes`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
study_genes <- tibble(
  gene_id  = c("ENSG00000141510", "ENSG00000012048", "ENSG00000146648",
               "ENSG00000139618", "ENSG00000157764"),
  log2FC   = c(-1.8, 2.4, 0.6, -3.1, 1.2),
  padj     = c(0.001, 0.032, 0.412, 0.0004, 0.018)
)

# Your code: fetch annotations and join
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
fetch_annotation <- function(ensembl_id) {
  resp <- request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", ensembl_id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_error(is_error = \(r) FALSE) |>
    req_perform()
  if (resp_status(resp) != 200L) return(tibble(gene_id = ensembl_id, symbol = NA_character_, biotype = NA_character_))
  g <- resp_body_json(resp)
  tibble(gene_id = g$id, symbol = g$display_name, biotype = g$biotype)
}

annotations <- map(study_genes$gene_id, fetch_annotation) |> list_rbind()

study_genes |>
  left_join(annotations, by = "gene_id") |>
  arrange(padj)
```
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 6: Pipeline Function with `possibly()`"}
Wrap the API fetch + clean + join workflow into a reusable function `annotate_genes(gene_ids)`. Use `purrr::possibly()` so that a failed lookup returns `NULL` instead of stopping the whole pipeline.

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Your code: define annotate_genes() using possibly() internally
# It should accept a character vector of Ensembl IDs and return a tibble
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
#| eval: false
.fetch_one <- function(ensembl_id) {
  resp <- request(paste0("https://rest.ensembl.org/lookup/id/", ensembl_id)) |>
    req_headers("Accept" = "application/json") |>
    req_throttle(rate = 5 / 1) |>
    req_error(is_error = \(r) FALSE) |>
    req_perform()
  if (resp_status(resp) != 200L) stop("HTTP ", resp_status(resp))
  g <- resp_body_json(resp)
  tibble(gene_id = g$id, symbol = g$display_name,
         biotype = g$biotype, chromosome = g$seq_region_name,
         start = g$start, end = g$end)
}

# possibly() returns NULL (not an error) on failure
.safe_fetch <- possibly(.fetch_one, otherwise = NULL)

annotate_genes <- function(gene_ids) {
  map(gene_ids, .safe_fetch) |>
    compact() |>          # drop NULLs (failed lookups)
    list_rbind()
}

# Test it
test_ids <- c("ENSG00000141510", "ENSG99999999999", "ENSG00000012048")
annotate_genes(test_ids)   # should return 2 rows, silently dropping the bad ID
```
:::

## Part 2: Tidyverse Cumulative Practice

### Practice Problem 1: Seasonal QC Summary

Find the season with the highest proportion of QC-passing samples.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
samples |>
  mutate(
    month_num = month(sequencing_date),
    season = case_when(
      month_num %in% 3:5  ~ "Spring",
      month_num %in% 6:8  ~ "Summer",
      month_num %in% 9:11 ~ "Fall",
      TRUE                ~ "Winter"
    )
  ) |>
  summarize(qc_pass_rate = mean(qc_pass), .by = season) |>
  arrange(desc(qc_pass_rate))
```

### Practice Problem 2: QC Pass Rate Heatmap by Batch and Tissue

Create a heatmap of QC pass rate by batch and tissue.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
samples |>
  summarize(pass_rate = mean(qc_pass), .by = c(batch, tissue)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = batch, y = tissue, fill = pass_rate)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "steelblue", labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "QC Pass Rate by Batch and Tissue", x = "Batch", y = "Tissue", fill = "Pass Rate") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Practice Problem 3: Read Count Ranking by Condition

Rank tissues by mean read count within each condition.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
samples |>
  summarize(mean_reads = mean(read_count_millions), .by = c(condition, tissue)) |>
  mutate(rank = min_rank(desc(mean_reads)), .by = condition) |>
  arrange(condition, rank)
```

### Practice Problem 4: Rolling 7-Day Sample Count

Plot the 7-day rolling count of sequencing runs (all tissues combined).

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4
daily_counts <- samples |>
  count(sequencing_date) |>
  arrange(sequencing_date) |>
  mutate(
    rolling_n = slider::slide_dbl(n, sum, .before = 6, .complete = TRUE)
  )

ggplot(daily_counts, aes(x = sequencing_date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = n), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(aes(y = rolling_n), color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Daily Sequencing Runs â€” 7-Day Rolling Total",
       x = NULL, y = "Sample count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Practice Problem 5: Per-Tissue Models

Fit a linear model `read_count_millions ~ rin_score` for each tissue. Report the slope and R-squared.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
samples |>
  nest(.by = tissue) |>
  mutate(
    model     = map(data, \(df) lm(read_count_millions ~ rin_score, data = df)),
    slope     = map_dbl(model, \(m) coef(m)["rin_score"]),
    r_squared = map_dbl(model, \(m) summary(m)$r.squared)
  ) |>
  select(tissue, slope, r_squared) |>
  arrange(desc(r_squared))
```

### Your Turn: Exercise 4

Find the day of the week with the highest total read count (in millions). Create a bar chart.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4
samples |>
  mutate(weekday = wday(sequencing_date, label = TRUE)) |>
  summarize(total_reads = sum(read_count_millions), .by = weekday) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = weekday, y = total_reads)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Total Read Count by Day of Week", x = NULL, y = "Total reads (M)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Your Turn: Exercise 5

For each tissue, find the month with the highest and lowest mean read count. Return a tibble with columns: tissue, best_month, worst_month.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
monthly <- samples |>
  mutate(month_name = month(sequencing_date, label = TRUE)) |>
  summarize(mean_reads = mean(read_count_millions), .by = c(tissue, month_name))

best  <- monthly |> slice_max(mean_reads, n = 1, by = tissue) |> select(tissue, best_month  = month_name)
worst <- monthly |> slice_min(mean_reads, n = 1, by = tissue) |> select(tissue, worst_month = month_name)

inner_join(best, worst, by = "tissue")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Using `group_by()` without `ungroup()`:

```r
samples |>
  group_by(tissue) |>
  mutate(pct_reads = read_count_millions / sum(read_count_millions))
# Next operation is still grouped! Can cause unexpected behavior.
```

Prefer `.by` in `summarize()` and `mutate()` to avoid forgotten groups.
:::

:::: {.callout-caution title="Your Turn â€” Exercise 8: Per-Tissue QC with `nest()` + `map()`"}
Using the `samples` tibble, fit a linear model `read_count_millions ~ rin_score` per tissue. Return a tibble with columns: `tissue`, `slope`, `r_squared`. Which tissue shows the strongest association between RIN score and read depth?

```{r}
#| eval: true
# Starter: nest() groups the data, then map() applies the model
samples |>
  nest(.by = tissue)
```

::::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{r}
samples |>
  nest(.by = tissue) |>
  mutate(
    model     = map(data, \(df) lm(read_count_millions ~ rin_score, data = df)),
    slope     = map_dbl(model, \(m) coef(m)["rin_score"]),
    r_squared = map_dbl(model, \(m) summary(m)$r.squared)
  ) |>
  select(tissue, slope, r_squared) |>
  arrange(desc(r_squared))
```
:::

## Part 3: End-to-End Pipeline

### The Messy Dataset

```{r}
messy <- tibble(
  order_date = c("2024-01-15", "2024/02/20", "March 5, 2024", "2024-04-10",
                 "2024-05-22", "2024-06-30", NA, "2024-08-15"),
  customer = c("alice smith", "BOB JONES", "Carlos M.", " diana Lee",
               "Elena Wu", "FRANK o'brien", "alice smith", "Bob Jones"),
  item = c("Widget_Pro", "gadget-basic", "WIDGET PRO", "Doohickey Ultra",
           "widget_pro", "Gadget Basic", "DOOHICKEY_ULTRA", "Widget Pro"),
  quantity = c("5", "three", "2", "10", "7", "4", "6", "8"),
  amount = c(149.95, 29.97, NA, 199.90, 209.65, 59.96, 179.94, 239.92),
  status = c("shipped", "DELIVERED", "Shipped", "pending", "SHIPPED",
             "Delivered", "cancelled", "PENDING")
)

messy
```

### Your Turn: Exercise 6 (Pipeline Project)

Clean this dataset. Your final tibble should have:

- `order_date` as a proper Date
- `customer` in title case with trimmed whitespace
- `item` cleaned to consistent format (title case, spaces instead of underscores/hyphens)
- `quantity` as numeric (handle the text "three" -> 3)
- `amount` with NAs replaced by the median
- `status` as a factor with levels: pending, shipped, delivered, cancelled

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
word_to_num <- function(x) {
  words <- c(one = 1, two = 2, three = 3, four = 4, five = 5,
             six = 6, seven = 7, eight = 8, nine = 9, ten = 10)
  x_lower <- str_to_lower(x)
  ifelse(x_lower %in% names(words), words[x_lower], suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x)))
}

clean <- messy |>
  mutate(
    order_date = parse_date_time(order_date, orders = c("ymd", "y/m/d", "mdy")) |> as_date(),
    customer   = str_trim(customer) |> str_to_title(),
    item       = str_replace_all(item, "[_-]", " ") |> str_to_title(),
    quantity   = word_to_num(quantity),
    amount     = coalesce(amount, median(amount, na.rm = TRUE)),
    status     = str_to_lower(status) |>
      factor(levels = c("pending", "shipped", "delivered", "cancelled"))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(order_date))

clean
```

### Your Turn: Exercise 7

Using your cleaned data from Exercise 6, answer:

1. How many orders per status?
2. Which customer has the highest total amount?
3. Which item is most popular by quantity?

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
# 1. Orders per status
clean |> count(status)

# 2. Highest total by customer
clean |>
  summarize(total = sum(amount), .by = customer) |>
  slice_max(total, n = 1)

# 3. Most popular item
clean |>
  summarize(total_qty = sum(quantity, na.rm = TRUE), .by = item) |>
  slice_max(total_qty, n = 1)
```

### Your Turn: Exercise 8 (Part 3 version)

Wrap your cleaning logic from Exercise 6 into a function called `clean_orders` that accepts a raw tibble and returns the cleaned version. Test it on the original `messy` data.

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
clean_orders <- function(raw) {
  word_to_num <- function(x) {
    words <- c(one = 1, two = 2, three = 3, four = 4, five = 5,
               six = 6, seven = 7, eight = 8, nine = 9, ten = 10)
    x_lower <- str_to_lower(x)
    ifelse(x_lower %in% names(words), words[x_lower], suppressWarnings(as.numeric(x)))
  }

  raw |>
    mutate(
      order_date = parse_date_time(order_date, orders = c("ymd", "y/m/d", "mdy")) |> as_date(),
      customer   = str_trim(customer) |> str_to_title(),
      item       = str_replace_all(item, "[_-]", " ") |> str_to_title(),
      quantity   = word_to_num(quantity),
      amount     = coalesce(amount, median(amount, na.rm = TRUE)),
      status     = str_to_lower(status) |>
        factor(levels = c("pending", "shipped", "delivered", "cancelled"))
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(order_date))
}

clean_orders(messy)
```

::: {.callout-note title="Reproducibility in Bioinformatics"}
In a real bioinformatics project, this pipeline would live in a `targets::tar_target()` so only changed steps re-run automatically. If your input data changes, `targets` detects the change and reruns only the downstream steps that depend on it. We cover `targets` in session 14.
:::

### Think About It

You receive a new batch of RNA-seq samples every week. Your PI wants a weekly QC report: pass rates by tissue, read depth distributions, and batch effect diagnostics. How would you structure your R project to handle this automatically? What would you put in functions vs. scripts vs. Quarto documents?

::: {.callout-caution title="(Optional Think-About-It) â€” httr2 vs jsonlite::fromJSON"}
**Why is `httr2::resp_body_json()` safer than `jsonlite::fromJSON(url)` directly?**

Think about what can go wrong at the network level: HTTP errors, timeouts, redirects, unexpected content types. Then compare what each approach does with those situations.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Answer Hint"}
`jsonlite::fromJSON(url)` makes a raw HTTP GET with no control over headers, no retry logic, no timeout, and no status-code checking. If the server returns a 429 (rate limit) or 503 (service unavailable), `fromJSON` may silently parse the error HTML as if it were data â€” or crash with a cryptic parse error.

`httr2::resp_body_json()` is always preceded by `req_perform()`, which:

- Checks the HTTP status code (errors on 4xx/5xx by default)
- Respects `Content-Type` headers
- Supports `req_throttle()`, `req_retry()`, `req_timeout()`
- Gives you `resp_status()`, `resp_headers()`, `resp_body_raw()` for debugging

Use `httr2` for any production or research code that queries external APIs.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Challenge â€” Bioinformatics Mini-Pipeline"}
Using the `samples` tibble and the `annotate_genes()` function from Exercise 6 (Part 1C), produce a final report tibble that merges sample-level QC metadata with gene-level API annotations.

Steps:

1. Filter `samples` to QC-passing rows only (`qc_pass == 1`).
2. Fetch annotations for a small gene panel (5 IDs of your choice).
3. Create a cross-join skeleton: one row per sample Ă— gene combination.
4. Add the annotation columns (symbol, biotype, chromosome) from step 2.
5. Add a mock `log2_expression` column drawn from `rnorm(n(), 0, 2)`.

This tests integration of all skills from today's session.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution sketch"
panel_ids <- c(
  "ENSG00000141510",  # TP53
  "ENSG00000012048",  # BRCA1
  "ENSG00000146648",  # EGFR
  "ENSG00000139618",  # BRCA2
  "ENSG00000157764"   # BRAF
)

# 1. QC-passing samples
qc_samples <- samples |> filter(qc_pass == 1L)

# 2. Gene annotations (using annotate_genes() from Exercise 7)
gene_annot <- annotate_genes(panel_ids)

# 3-5. Cross-join and add mock expression
set.seed(42)
qc_samples |>
  cross_join(gene_annot) |>
  mutate(log2_expression = rnorm(n(), mean = 0, sd = 2))
```
:::

## Session Synthesis

### Connect the Dots

Build a full pipeline that:

1. Starts from the `samples` RNA-seq metadata tibble
2. Cleans and summarizes QC metrics per tissue and batch
3. Joins with per-tissue model results
4. Produces a summary report with a visualization

```{r}
performance <- tibble(
  tissue    = c("liver", "kidney", "brain"),
  q1_score  = c(92, 78, 88),
  q2_score  = c(88, 82, 91),
  q3_score  = c(95, 80, 85),
  q4_score  = c(90, 85, 89)
)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Solution"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

# 1. Summarize QC per tissue
tissue_qc <- samples |>
  summarize(
    n_samples       = n(),
    pass_rate       = mean(qc_pass),
    mean_reads      = mean(read_count_millions),
    mean_rin        = mean(rin_score),
    .by = tissue
  )

# 2. Join with quarterly performance scores
full_data <- tissue_qc |>
  left_join(performance, by = "tissue")

# 3. Pivot-longer approach â€” scalable, no rowwise()
full_data <- full_data |>
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("q"), names_to = "quarter", values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(quarter = str_remove(quarter, "_score") |> str_to_upper())

# 4. Summary table
full_data |>
  summarize(
    avg_score  = mean(score),
    pass_rate  = first(pass_rate),
    mean_reads = first(mean_reads),
    .by = tissue
  )

# 5. Visualization
full_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = quarter, y = score, color = tissue, group = tissue)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Quarterly QC Scores by Tissue",
       x = "Quarter", y = "Score", color = "Tissue") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.callout-important title="Wide Matrices: Avoid `rowwise()`, Use `pivot_longer()`"}
The `rowwise() |> mutate(avg = mean(c_across(...)))` pattern works for tiny data frames but is painfully slow on wide expression matrices (thousands of genes x hundreds of samples). For every row R calls `mean()` separately â€” that is thousands of function calls instead of one vectorized operation.

The fast, idiomatic alternative:

```r
# Slow (rowwise)
df |> rowwise() |> mutate(avg = mean(c_across(starts_with("q"))))

# Fast (pivot_longer + summarize)
df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("q"), names_to = "quarter", values_to = "score") |>
  summarize(avg = mean(score), .by = id_column)
```

Get used to going long. Wide is for display; long is for computation.
:::

### Key Takeaways

- Three tools, in order of cost: `rvest` (static HTML) â†’ `RSelenium` (JS-rendered pages) â†’ `httr2` (REST API). Reach for the cheapest one that does the job.
- `rvest::read_html()` + a CSS selector + `html_table()` / `html_text2()` covers nearly every static scraping need.
- `RSelenium` drives a real browser; the pattern is *render with the browser, parse the rendered HTML with rvest*. Always close the browser in a `tryCatch(..., finally = ...)`.
- REST APIs (Ensembl, UniProt, NCBI, ChEMBL) are stable, versioned, and structured â€” prefer them whenever they exist.
- `httr2` provides explicit error handling, throttling, and retry logic that bare `fromJSON(url)` calls lack; `purrr::pluck()` and `keep()` make nested JSON navigation readable.
- Real data requires cleaning. Expect inconsistent text, dates, types, and missing values.
- For wide data summaries, `pivot_longer()` + `group_by()` scales to thousands of genes; `rowwise()` does not.
- A good data pipeline is a function: raw data in, analysis-ready data out.

### Prepare for Next Session

Session 11 introduces Quarto. You will learn to turn your R analysis into polished HTML documents, PDFs, and Word files. Review how code chunks work (you have been using them in every workbook). Bring a short analysis you would like to turn into a report.
